items.headline: Google Bets on AI-Powered Shopping as Retailers Face Loss of Control
items.body:
Google is building infrastructure to let AI agents handle shopping decisions on behalf of users, fundamentally shifting how consumers discover and purchase products online. At Google I/O, the company announced a Universal Cart that aggregates items across retailers and Google services—Search, Gemini, YouTube, and Gmail—with AI assistance tracking prices, suggesting discounts, and flagging incompatible purchases. The move signals Google's confidence that autonomous shopping agents represent the next major commerce frontier, even as competitors like OpenAI and Perplexity have faced setbacks in checkout functionality.

## Google's Shopping Architecture

The Universal Cart consolidates purchases scattered across devices and platforms into a single interface that runs on Gemini. Items added while browsing or chatting can be checked out through Google, with the AI providing price-history alerts, in-stock notifications, and payment recommendations through connected loyalty programs and credit cards. The cart also handles technical validation—flagging incompatible PC components, for example—reducing friction in complex purchases.

Google has integrated major retailers including Sephora, Target, Wayfair, and Walmart. The company developed the Universal Commerce Protocol (UCP) with these partners in January, establishing an open standard that enables seamless AI shopping journeys. Notably, Google says it currently takes no commission on sales, positioning itself as a "matchmaker" rather than merchant of record. Users retain the option to complete checkout on retailer websites instead.

## The Structural Threat to Retail

Google's infrastructure creates what industry observers call "the Doordash problem"—customers bypass retailers' websites entirely, accessing products exclusively through intermediaries. Amazon already sued AI company Perplexity in November over similar shopping functionality, signaling how seriously e-commerce platforms view this dynamic. If agentic shopping gains adoption, retailers lose direct customer relationships, data collection opportunities, and the ability to market additional products at checkout.

This structural shift explains why retailer participation is essential and fragile. Merchants benefit from exposure through Google's platforms but risk becoming commoditized, competing purely on price and inventory. The tension is real: widespread adoption of AI shopping agents could shrink merchant margins while concentrating customer relationships at search and payment layers. For enterprises building AI commerce tools, the takeaway is clear—success requires solving the incentive problem between platforms, retailers, and consumers. Sustainable agentic shopping depends on demonstrating genuine value for all parties, not simply extracting friction from existing supply chains.
items.original_title: Would you let robots spend your money? Google is betting on it

items.headline: Google Search Adds AI Agents, Custom Widgets, Multimodal Input
items.body:
Google is restructuring Search around AI-native workflows, rolling out a unified search interface that collapses the distinction between traditional results, AI-generated summaries, and conversational AI modes. The shift, announced at Google I/O 2026, aims to reduce friction between different search experiences and embed AI agents directly into the platform as active research tools rather than passive answer engines.

## Search Interface Consolidation and Agent Capabilities

Google's redesigned search box, powered by Gemini 3.5 Flash, now expands for longer queries and includes AI-powered autocomplete that builds on user input. The interface seamlessly bridges AI Overviews—AI-generated summaries appearing at the top of results—and AI Mode, Google's conversational search experience. Users can attach documents, photos, videos, and Chrome tabs directly to queries without switching modes. The unified box rolls out globally on desktop and mobile starting this week, though users can still access traditional web results via a separate tab.

More significantly, Google is introducing AI information agents for AI Pro and AI Ultra subscribers. These agents monitor specified topics in the background and notify users of updates—tracking concert dates, price changes, or other monitored topics while the user is offline. Agents integrate with other Google services through Personal Intelligence, pulling contextual data from Gmail and other apps. Google is also expanding its AI-powered booking feature to schedule local services and make calls on behalf of users to businesses like pet groomers and repair services.

## The Shift From Retrieval to Task Execution

The moves signal a fundamental recalibration of Search from a retrieval tool toward an autonomous task platform. Agents that research without user prompts represent a departure from on-demand query answering—the core function of Search for nearly three decades. The addition of "generative UI" features allowing custom widget and app creation within Search extends the platform further into application development territory.

For enterprise AI practitioners, the direction matters: Google is betting that search relevance increasingly depends on autonomous monitoring, contextual integration across services, and the ability to execute real-world tasks like scheduling and booking. The requirement that agents connect to multiple Google accounts for personalization also signals growing integration of user data across Google's ecosystem as a competitive advantage. Organizations relying on search-adjacent AI workflows should assess how these autonomous agents and multimodal inputs might reshape information discovery workflows and task automation in their environments.
items.original_title: Google Search is getting its biggest changes ever
